editorialReading: The US has structural advantages here — CF Industries' domestic ammonia and urea capacity at Donaldsonville and Yazoo City, plus the Tampa contract benchmark — but Brazil and Mexico remain heavily import-exposed via Gulf flows, and US grain exports are the Western Hemisphere's stabiliser. The squeeze on hemisphere food security comes less from American farmer pump-side prices and more from import-dependent neighbours losing access to affordable nitrogen at exactly the wrong moment. That is already visible: global urea has roughly doubled (Egypt FOB ~$800–850/t) while Brazilian potash offers around $370–440/t CFR ahead of the Safrinha — the import-dependent half of the hemisphere is squeezed on both nitrogen and potash at once.
watchNext: The May World Bank Pink Sheet has now landed, confirming the Hormuz-driven surge — the fertilizer index rose about 14% in April and urea hit a four-year high. Watch the June Pink Sheet, Argus weekly summaries and IFA Q2 commentary for whether the spike extends or rolls over, plus any further India / China export restrictions. Aramco CEO Amin Nasser's mid-June restoration timeline remains the implicit benchmark — if Hormuz flows aren't substantially restored by then, the Northern Hemisphere autumn fertilizer buying window will close with prices locked in at crisis levels.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
